lead_title: Cursor is consolidating the AI developer tooling stack — from IDE to version control to acquisition target — signaling a platform power grab in the coding layer

options.conviction: L2: Platform risk in AI developer tools is now existential — teams using Cursor face compounding lock-in as SpaceX ownership changes incentive structures while Origin creates proprietary version control dependency
options.post:
SpaceX owns your editor. When did that stop feeling dangerous?

Every team I talk to uses Cursor for daily development.

Same week SpaceX bought it for $60 billion, they announced Origin, Git rebuilt for AI agents.

Now one space company controls where you code AND how you version control.

The mechanism here is compounding lock-in.

Teams switched to Cursor because AI coding was genuinely better. Fair trade.

But Origin changes the game. When your version control only works perfectly with their editor, switching costs multiply. Your team's muscle memory, custom configs, debugging patterns, all tied to one vendor.

At Atlan, we've been building agents for months and this tracks.

The difference between AI tools and platform risk is who owns the workflow when the tools become commodity.

SpaceX will optimize Cursor for SpaceX's aerospace goals first. Enterprise pricing, roadmap decisions, API access, all serve their $26 trillion ambitions.

Independent teams become second-class users in their own primary tool.

Platform risk in developer tools is now identical to mobile app store risk. When Apple changes rules, apps break. When Cursor/SpaceX change APIs, entire development workflows break.

Most Cursor users still think they're using an independent tool.

What's your switching cost if they change pricing tomorrow?

options.conviction: L1: AI market fragmentation forces teams to architect for multi-vendor dependencies — ChatGPT losing majority share signals the end of single-platform API strategies
options.post:
ChatGPT hit 1.1 billion users. And lost majority market share.

1.1 billion users is 47% of the total AI market now.

Gemini: 662 million users. Claude: 245 million and growing fast.

Every week I watch builders debate which API to standardize on.

The answer used to be obvious. OpenAI was the only game that mattered.

Not anymore.

18 months ago, teams could build on OpenAI's API and know they were using the market leader. Single-vendor strategies felt safe.

Now the market is genuinely competitive. No single platform is safe to depend on exclusively.

This changes everything for product teams:
- Which API for customer support?
- Which for code generation?
- Which for data analysis?

The answer increasingly is "all of them."

I build AI agents at Atlan and we're already architecting for provider switching from day one. API abstraction layers, multiple provider keys, task-based routing.

The fragmentation is accelerating faster than most teams realize. Enterprise teams are diversifying AI dependencies because they can't bet everything on one vendor anymore.

ChatGPT losing majority share signals the end of single-vendor AI architecture.

Teams that built on single APIs will retrofit. Teams building new products will architect for provider switching from the start.

What's your backup plan when your primary AI provider changes pricing?

options.conviction: L2: AI is graduating from marketing buzzword to invisible infrastructure controlled by platform giants — consumer AI fatigue and developer platform consolidation are the same phenomenon
options.post:
60% of consumers hate AI branding. SpaceX just bought Cursor.

Two facts that landed the same week.

Both signal the same shift.

The AI bubble is hardening into infrastructure controlled by a few players.

SpaceX bought Cursor for the workflow lock-in. Cursor + Origin means controlling how millions of developers build software.

Meanwhile, consumers are rejecting "AI-powered" marketing. 60% find it off-putting.

The pattern: AI is graduating from marketing buzzword to utility infrastructure.

When water becomes a utility, nobody brags about being "water-powered."

I see this across my network. Teams that shipped AI features 18 months ago now describe them as "automation" or "intelligent workflows."

The AI label became a liability, not a feature.

Same week Platform risk became existential for development teams, consumer trust in AI messaging collapsed.

This isn't coincidence.

We're watching AI disappear into the stack. The companies winning are the ones controlling the pipes.

SpaceX owns your editor. Microsoft owns your models. Google owns your search.

The AI layer is becoming invisible infrastructure owned by giants.

Builders who recognized this early pivoted from "AI startup" to "productivity tool." They dropped AI from landing pages and focused on job-to-be-done messaging.

The ones still leading with AI features are competing for a shrinking market of early adopters.

What job does your product actually do when you remove the AI label?
